Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic but I made it work. I'd usually listen to lecture recordings during my commute or while doing chores since I learn best by hearing things. With 19 online courses to juggle, I had to be creative about when I could fit studying in. I'd batch my study sessions on weekends mostly, spending maybe 3-4 hours at a time when I could focus. It wasn't perfect, but I found my rhythm eventually.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying a few hours each day, but then life would throw something at me and I'd have to cram everything into a couple of days. Having limited resources means I sometimes had to prioritize work or family stuff over a perfectly regular study schedule. I learned to be flexible and not beat myself up when things didn't go according to plan.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about new content being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed since missing important info stresses me out. I also made it a habit to check in before the weekend so I could plan what I needed to download and study offline. The anxiety of falling behind was honestly a big motivator for me to stay somewhat connected.
Q4: It took a while, but eventually checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic for me. I'd do it while having breakfast, just a quick scroll to see what was new or what deadlines were approaching. The mindfulness exercises I started doing actually helped me create more intentional routines, including my study habits. Once I connected it to something I was already doing every day, it stuck better.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I'd watch them multiple times, sometimes just listening while doing other things to really absorb the concepts. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives and questions often clarified things I was confused about. When someone explained something in a different way in the discussions, it would sometimes click for me in a way the original lecture didn't.
Q6: I'll be honest, I barely touched the reading materials and PDFs because reading for long periods is harder for me to retain. The supplementary resources often felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to keep up with the core content. I also tended to skip the practice quizzes unless they were graded because I was always short on time. It wasn't that I didn't value them, I just had to prioritize what would directly impact my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be tested on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. I also valued anything I could do while multitasking since my schedule is so packed. If something felt like busywork or I couldn't see the point, my motivation to complete it dropped significantly. The discussions felt worthwhile because the peer interaction made me feel less alone in this.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With required work already piling up and my assignment completion being what it was, optional stuff got pushed to the bottom of the list. There was also this mental thing where if it wasn't required, my anxious brain would say 'skip it, focus on what counts.' I wish I'd had more time for optional enrichment, but survival mode doesn't leave much room for extras.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture audio files and listen to them repeatedly on my phone when I didn't have good internet. I also made voice memos summarizing key concepts and would play those back to myself. My notes were all in a physical notebook because I couldn't always afford to be online, and I'd study from those extensively.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be lying in bed listening to downloaded lectures or my own recorded summaries, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also have study sessions with a friend where we'd quiz each other verbally on the material. Sometimes I'd spend hours just thinking through concepts while doing other tasks, like working through problems in my head during my commute. The LMS only sees when I'm clicking, not when I'm actually learning.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video once to download it, then watch it three or four more times offline over the next few days. Same with any materials I could save - I'd grab them when I had good internet access and then study them whenever I could. My actual engagement with the content was way higher than my access logs would suggest because most of my repeated studying happened offline.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything seemed due at once and my anxiety spiked hard. Financial stress would sometimes make it impossible to focus on coursework because I was worried about more immediate survival stuff. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd sometimes just freeze and not engage with anything for a few days. But my resilience kicked in eventually - I'd do some mindfulness exercises, remind myself I've gotten through hard things before, and push forward.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those things affected my LMS use constantly. When I was exhausted from working or dealing with family responsibilities, I'd only do the bare minimum online. Confusion about course material sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how lost I felt. The fatigue was real - some days I'd log in, stare at the screen, and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. That's when I learned to step back and try again when I was in a better headspace.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be racing with anxiety about other things. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't really processing any of it. There's a difference between having your eyes on content and actually engaging your brain with it. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I was too anxious to stop and too unfocused to actually learn.
Q15: I postponed assignments a lot, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was anxiety - the bigger and more important something felt, the scarier it was to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it when I felt more prepared or less stressed, but that perfect moment rarely came. Sometimes I avoided things because I was afraid of failing at them, which is ironic because avoiding them made failure more likely. It's something I'm still working on.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be relatively quiet on the platform until about 48 hours before something was due, then suddenly I'd be logging in constantly. It wasn't ideal, but the pressure of a deadline was often the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and get me moving. I became very good at deadline sprints, even if my assignment scores suffered from not having more time to refine things.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me when I actually got it. Positive feedback would boost my motivation for days and make me more likely to engage with the next assignment. Critical feedback was harder - sometimes it would trigger my anxiety and make me avoid that area of the course for a while. But ultimately, knowing someone was actually looking at my work made me feel more connected to the course and more motivated to try.
Q18: The course structure was a bit overwhelming with so many modules and resources available. I appreciated having flexibility in when I could access things, but sometimes too much flexibility meant I'd procrastinate. The weekly rhythm of new content helped me stay somewhat on track. I wish there had been clearer guidance on what was truly essential versus nice-to-have, because I wasted energy trying to figure out what to prioritize.
Q19: Having video content was essential for my learning style, so that made engagement much easier. The discussion forums being active helped me feel less isolated. What made it harder was when instructions were only in text form - I'd have to read them multiple times to understand. Also, when there were too many small tasks scattered around, I'd lose track of things and my anxiety would spike. Simpler, clearer organization would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways - logging in frequently to check things, watching videos, reading discussions, but struggling to actually complete and submit work. The completion part requires a different kind of energy than just consuming content. Maybe they're dealing with anxiety or perfectionism that makes it hard to submit imperfect work. Or they're learning a lot but struggling with the specific format assignments require.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like my situation. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion was low and my LMS activity wasn't super high. I was learning effectively offline through listening to recordings and verbal review. The LMS doesn't capture all the ways people study, especially auditory learners like me who don't need to be clicking around to be absorbing information. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone tends to be active, whether they're consistent or deadline-driven, if they're engaging with discussions. But they miss so much of the actual learning. They can't measure understanding, they can't see offline study, they can't capture the quality of engagement versus just clicking through. They also can't show the emotional journey - the anxiety, the breakthroughs, the moments of genuine connection with the material.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the course material, wrestling with ideas, and trying to connect new concepts to what I already know. It's not about how many times I log in or how long I watch videos. Real engagement is when I'm walking down the street and suddenly understand something that confused me earlier. It's when I want to discuss the material with someone because I find it genuinely interesting. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time spent.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I relied on audio learning and offline study - that's probably half my actual engagement right there. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices that helped me manage my anxiety enough to actually focus on coursework. It can't show the peer support I got from friends outside the platform, or the personal growth I experienced in building resilience through a challenging semester. My journey through this course was about so much more than clicks and timestamps.
